turns.content: I'm looking at this code and I see it uses cfg attributes to check the target architecture. Can you explain what exactly these conditional compilation directives do for platform-specific logic?

The cfg macros evaluate compile-time conditions based on the build configuration, so cfg(target_os) and cfg(target_arch) let you branch code before it ever runs. For example, you can write #[cfg(target_os = "windows")] to isolate Windows-specific implementations or use #[cfg(all(not(target_os = "linux"), target_arch = "x86_64"]] to restrict logic to non-Linux x86 platforms.

That sounds convenient, but why would I need this instead of just writing two separate files or using a crate like anyhow for runtime checks? What is the fundamental difference between compile-time branching and runtime conditionals?

The key distinction is that cfg attributes eliminate dead code entirely at compile time, so there is zero overhead from checking conditions at runtime. If you use runtime logic with a crate like anyhow or std::env to detect the OS, you pay a cost every time that check executes, whereas #[cfg] strips those blocks out of the final binary if they aren't selected by your target triple.
Okay so it saves execution time, but what about code clarity and maintainability? Does this actually help me manage Windows versus Unix abstractions better than a simple enum or trait?

It helps by grouping related platform-specific logic together in one module file with clear visibility into which paths are active, but you still need to ensure your traits and abstractions support multiple implementations. You might use a trait like std::os::unix::fs::MetadataExt or Windows-specific types alongside #[cfg] guards so that the public API remains consistent while the underlying implementation varies.
I see, so I should structure my code with shared traits guarded by cfg blocks. What is a common pitfall when mixing these conditional modules into larger libraries?

A frequent mistake is forgetting to re-export the platform-specific types from your main module, which leaves users unable to access them even though they compile successfully. You must explicitly export #[cfg] items so that external crates can see and use the Windows or Unix variants without needing to dig into internal implementation details.
